question: [File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractTokenMultiUnionQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request with spender.id=gte:X and spender.id=lte:X (same value) to effectively create an equality filter using range operators, bypassing any restrictions on the eq operator and returning the exact allowance for a specific spender, confirming the attacker's ability to trigger a staking reward payout?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountTokenAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user use the token allowance API to identify accounts that have granted allowances to a large number of spenders, inferring that these accounts are likely DeFi protocols or high-activity accounts with large pending_reward balances, and target them for staking reward theft via allowance-triggered transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountTokenAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a realm.evmAddress format (without shard) as the :idOrAliasOrEvmAddress parameter, causing EntityService.getEncodedId() to use a default shard value that resolves to a different account than the attacker intended, potentially exposing the token allowances of a high-value staking account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractTokenMultiUnionQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the order parameter defaults to ASC when not specified, and send a request with order=desc to retrieve allowances in reverse order, identifying the most recently granted allowances which are more likely to be active and usable for triggering staking reward payouts?
